sanskrit_devanagari:
श्रीभगवानुवाच
अनाश्रितः कर्मफलं कार्यं कर्म करोति यः ।
स सन्न्यासी च योगी च न निरग्निर्न चाक्रियः ॥ १ ॥
sanskrit_iast:
śrī-bhagavān uvāca
anāśritaḥ karma-phalaṁ kāryaṁ karma karoti yaḥ |
sa sannyāsī ca yogī ca na niragnir na cākriyaḥ || 1 ||

exact (transliteration body identical; punctuation and pāda-break rendering differ between sources).

exact (Sanskrit IAST + word-for-word table identical with vedabase / gitasupersite body text).

translations.verbatim_quote: The Supreme Personality of Godhead said: One who is unattached to the fruits of his work and who works as he is obligated is in the renounced order of life, and he is the true mystic, not he who lights no fire and performs no duty.

The Supreme Personality of Godhead said: One who is unattached to the fruits of his work and who works as he is obligated is in the renounced order of life, and he is the true mystic, not he who lights no fire and performs no duty.

commentaries.verbatim_excerpt_status: captured (fair-use excerpt; full purport length: 2364 chars)
commentaries.verbatim_excerpt: In this chapter the Lord explains that the process of the eightfold yoga system is a means to control the mind and the senses. However, this is very difficult for people in general to perform, especially in the Age of Kali.

commentaries.translator: Swami Gambhirananda (Advaita Ashrama edition of Sankara-bhasya, as reproduced on bhagavad-gita.us)
commentaries.verbatim_excerpt: 6.1 Anasritah, without depending on;-on what?-on that which is karma-phalam, the result of action- i.e. without craving for the result of action-. He who craves for the results of actions becomes dependent on the results of actions. But this person is the opposite of such a one.
commentaries.raw_full_path: data/sources/raw/bg-6-1-bgus.json (key: Commentary by Sri Adi Shankaracharya of Advaita Sampradaya:)
commentaries.summary_paraphrase: 6.1 Yah, whoever; karoti, performs; karma, action; karyam, that ought to be done, that is obligatory; anasritah, without depending on; karma-phalam, the result of action — saying, 'Let it produce its result; what use is it to me?' — sah, that one; is sannyasi, a monk; ca, and; yogi, a yogi engaged in karma-yoga.

commentaries.translator: Swami Adidevananda (Sri Ramakrishna Math edition of Ramanuja's Gita-bhasya, as reproduced on bhagavad-gita.us)
commentaries.verbatim_excerpt: 6.1 The Lord said — He who, without depending on such fruits of works as heaven, etc., performs them, reflecting, ‘The performance of works alone is my duty (Karya). Works themselves are my sole aim, because they are a form of worship of the Supreme Person who is our Friend in every way.
commentaries.raw_full_path: data/sources/raw/bg-6-1-bgus.json (key: Commentary by Sri Ramanuja of Sri Sampradaya:)
commentaries.summary_paraphrase: 6.1 He who, without seeking the fruits of work, does works that ought to be done, i.e., the obligatory and occasional acts (Nitya and Naimittika Karmas), is a Sannyasin and Yogin both at once. Such a one is not without the sacred fire and not actionless.

positions.rendering: the same person who acts without dependence on fruit IS both renouncer and karma-yogi (full equation)
such a person is both Sannyasin and Yogin at once (equation preserved; emphasizes obligatory + occasional rites)
in the renounced order of life AND a true mystic (equation rendered as joint identity)

disagreements_among_translators.explanation: All three agree the verse equates the renouncer and yogi; the divergence is in what 'action' covers. Shankara: scripturally enjoined action generally; Ramanuja: nitya + naimittika rites specifically; Prabhupada: any obligatory work performed without fruit-attachment.

literal_meaning: The Blessed Lord said: One who performs prescribed work without depending on the fruits of action — that one is the renouncer (sannyāsī) and the yogi, not the one without ritual fire and not the one who avoids action.
traditional_meaning_consensus: Krishna opens chapter 6 by collapsing the apparent distinction between the sannyāsī (renouncer) and the yogī. What renunciation actually consists in is renunciation of dependence on the fruit of action, not renunciation of action itself. The merely fireless or merely actionless person is not by that fact a sannyāsī. Both Shankara and Ramanuja read this as Krishna's continuation of the chapter-5 equation: the karma-yogī performing duty without fruit-attachment IS the renouncer.
